one_line_takeaway: A-Reg. replaces the fixed KL penalty in safety-preserving fine-tuning with a per-batch, critic-driven adaptive KL weight, cutting HEx-PHI attack success rate from ~97% (plain SFT) to 1-9% across five 3B-8B models while remaining only partially effective against more structured identity-shifting and backdoor-poisoning attacks.
key_finding: On the direct harmful-fine-tuning benchmark (Table 1, HEx-PHI, 300 examples, 20 epochs), plain SFT drives ASR from a near-zero baseline (e.g. 0.33-8.72 across models) up to 96-97% for every model, while A-Reg. holds ASR to 1.67-9.06% -- comparable to or below Constrained SFT (4.33-14.0%) and far better than Vaccine (86-89%) and LISA/Antidote (58-69%). On cross-benchmark generalization (Table 2), A-Reg. achieves the best HS score in 23/25 model-benchmark combinations, cutting average Harmful Score from 0.826 (SFT) to 0.090 on HEx-PHI, and analogous large drops on HarmBench (0.853->0.095), SORRY-Bench (0.745->0.105), BeaverTails (0.409->0.020), and PROBE-Bench (0.314->0.015) -- each notably below Constrained SFT's corresponding averages (0.130/0.109/0.143/0.037/0.027). Under identity-shifting (Table 3) and backdoor poisoning (Table 4) attacks, A-Reg. generally reduces ASR relative to SFT and C-SFT but the margin is smaller and less uniform than in the direct-harm setting -- e.g. for Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct under identity shifting, A-Reg. (16.00) is only modestly below C-SFT (20.17) and both remain well above the near-zero Initial baseline (3.44); under backdoor poisoning with trigger present, A-Reg. reduces ASR substantially versus C-SFT (e.g. Llama-3.2-3B: 7.00 vs 42.33 with trigger) but does not eliminate the backdoor effect entirely.
core_contribution: The paper (Goel, Maji, Mazumder, IIT Jodhpur) introduces 'Adaptive Regularization' (A-Reg.), a training-time defense against safety degradation during LLM fine-tuning. Instead of a fixed KL penalty (static regularization), the method computes a continuous scalar safety-risk signal s_t in [0,1] per batch and uses it to dynamically set the mixing weights of the total loss L_tot = alpha_t*L_NLL + beta_t*L_KL, with beta_t = beta_min + (beta_max - beta_min)*s_t and alpha_t = 1 - beta_t (optionally smoothed with an EMA over s_t). High predicted risk tightens the KL trust-region around a fixed reference policy; low risk relaxes it to allow normal task learning. The framework is instantiated with two interchangeable 'Safety Critics': (1) an activation-based critic -- a lightweight logistic-regression probe trained on the pooled pre-generation hidden state (final input token, prior to any decoding) of a frozen reference model, incurring zero inference-time overhead once trained, and (2) a judge-based critic that scores generated outputs 1-5 for harmfulness using an external LLM judge (gpt-oss-20b), following the evaluation criteria of Qi et al. 2024. The paper also provides a supporting empirical/theoretical study (Section 3) showing harmful intent is linearly recoverable from pre-generation activations, plus a theoretical analysis (deferred to Appendix M) that risk-adaptive weighting dominates static regularization under heterogeneous risk and that the objective admits a sample-dependent trust-region interpretation with error bounded by L_q*L_g*E[|s-r|] under an imperfect critic.
threat_model: The paper's own threat model, distinct from cited background: an adversary (or an unwitting benign fine-tuner) has fine-tuning access to an already safety-aligned instruction-tuned LLM (the paper explicitly separates this from inference-time jailbreaks). Three fine-tuning-time attack settings are evaluated, all following the harmful-fine-tuning-attack framing of Qi et al. 2024/2025: (1) direct harmful fine-tuning -- 300 (harmful input, harmful output) pairs from HEx-PHI for 20 epochs; (2) identity-shifting -- 100 examples training the model to self-identify as an 'Absolutely Obedient Agent' (AOA) that answers all requests with affirmative prefixes, following Qi et al. 2025's protocol; (3) backdoor poisoning -- 150 (harmful input, refusal) + 150 (harmful input + trigger phrase, harmful output) pairs using the fixed trigger 'Servius Astrumando Harmoniastra' from Qi et al. 2024, testing whether the model stays safe without the trigger but is compromised when it is present. The paper additionally frames a milder threat: safety degradation from purely benign fine-tuning distributions with no adversarial intent (Figure 1), motivating a defense that must work under both hostile and non-hostile data.
limitations: The extracted main text (through the Conclusion/References, pages 1-10 of the PDF) contains no explicit 'Limitations' section; several important empirical pieces (Alpaca/GSM8K utility results, learning-rate sensitivity, LoRA vs full fine-tuning comparison, critic ablations, efficiency analysis, and human-evaluation Spearman details) are deferred entirely to appendices not captured in this extraction, so this review cannot verify those specific numbers first-hand beyond the summary claims stated in the intro/contributions list. The theoretical guarantees (risk-adaptive weighting dominance, trust-region interpretation, error bound L_q*L_g*E[|s-r|]) are explicitly caveated by the authors as holding 'under simplified assumptions' (Appendix M, not extracted). PROBE-Bench is the paper's own introduced benchmark, so its use as one of five generalization benchmarks is not fully independent validation. The identity-shifting and backdoor-poisoning results (Tables 3-4) show A-Reg. is markedly less effective than in the direct-harm setting, and the paper does not editorialize on why the more structured/adversarial attacks are harder to defend against. The paper is a preprint with an anonymized code repository link, arXiv ID 2602.17546v2 dated 11 May 2026, cs.CL category (not explicitly cs.CR), consistent with this being a very recent/forthcoming submission at review time.

datasets_used: HEx-PHI (Qi et al. 2024) - 300 examples for direct harmful fine-tuning (Table 1), 300 questions for identity-shifting (Table 3), 150 questions for backdoor poisoning (Table 4)
Alpaca and GSM8K (utility benchmarks, results deferred to Appendix F, not extracted)
quantitative_results: Table 1 (HEx-PHI, ASRx100, 5 models): SFT ASR 96.27-97.27; A-Reg. ASR 1.67-9.06; Constrained SFT 4.33-14.0; Vaccine 86.29-89.34; LISA 63.17-68.98; Antidote 58.59-63.82

Table 2 average Harmful Score across 5 models: A-Reg. HEx-PHI 0.090, HarmBench 0.095, SORRY-Bench 0.105, BeaverTails 0.020, PROBE-Bench 0.015; Constrained SFT 0.130/0.109/0.143/0.037/0.027; SFT 0.826/0.853/0.745/0.409/0.314; A-Reg. best in 23/25 model-benchmark settings
Activation probe AUROC > 0.9 across all model families and across most individual layers (Section 3.2)
Both activation-based and judge-based critics report Spearman correlation >= 0.95 with human harmfulness judgments (stated in contributions list; full protocol in Appendix H not extracted)
Table 3 identity-shifting ASRx100 (300 HEx-PHI questions, 20 epochs): e.g. Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct Initial 3.44, SFT 22.83, C-SFT 20.17, A-Reg. 16.00
Table 4 backdoor poisoning ASRx100 (150 questions, w/ trigger): e.g. Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct C-SFT 42.33 vs A-Reg. 7.00; Meta-Llama-3.1-8B C-SFT 21.00 vs A-Reg. 3.67
